title: Loins Girded, Lamps Burning
subtitle: Waiting through the night for the Master's return

stillness: He who watches holds open the eyes of his mind to the sight of the true light.
Drive from yourself the darkness of the body and of negligence.
gospel: and your lamps burning in your hands.
And you yourselves be like men
when he shall return from the wedding;
that when he comes and knocks,
they may open to him at once.
whom the Lord, when he comes, shall find watching.
behold: Consider what the girded loins and the burning lamps are.
The loins of the mind are the will and the understanding,
from which proceed evil thoughts and depraved desires.
The loins of the flesh are those from which come carnal cravings and shameless deeds.
Both must be girded — drawn tight, held back from unlawful thoughts and works.
The girdle of the first is the constant meditation of the divine Law.
The girdle of the second is justice, wrought by the rigor of discipline.
The lamps are clear and shining teachings, and good examples born of love,
which must be in our hands, that we may fulfill them in deed:
burning, by the fervor of eternal charity;
shining, by the splendor of holy living —
as John was a lamp, burning and shining.
consider: First gird the loins, and then light the lamp.
First turn away from evil, and then do good.
First cease to act perversely, and then learn to do well.
For, as Augustine says, God does not bring His good things upon us unless He takes away our evil;
and His goods increase only insofar as ours are diminished,
nor will the one be perfected unless the other be ended.
He came to the wedding when, rising from the dead and ascending into heaven, the new Man joined to Himself the multitude of angels.
He returns when He is made known to us through judgment.
He knocks when, through the troubles of sickness, He shows that death is near.
We open to Him at once if we receive Him with fear.
He who dreads to go forth from the body does not wish to open to the knocking Judge,
and fears to see the One he remembers he despised.
But he who is secure in his hope and his works opens at once to the One who knocks,
for he gladly awaits the Judge;
and when he learns the time of approaching death, he rejoices in the glory of the resurrection.
Such a one can say with the Apostle: I desire to be dissolved, and to be with Christ.
He is happier than all the happy of this world, and more royal than kings.
And when the Lord comes, He will gird Himself,
and make them watching ones sit down,
and passing, He will minister to them:
He will give them the milk of His humanity and the wine of His Godhead,
and sweetest manna, which has not entered into the heart of man.
prayer: who commanded us both to bind fast our loins and to hold our lamps burning,
do You Yourself gird our loins,
that no iniquity may rule over us, and that we may turn aside to no sin.
Do You Yourself make our lamps burn, and guard them burning,
that our charity may not grow cold,
but that our every thought, word, and work
may always be directed to the doing of Your justice.
Make me, Lord, to await Your coming watchfully and earnestly,
that, receiving You with joy, I may be able — by Your mercy —
to come to the joys eternal. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Prius ergo præcinge lumbos, et tunc accende lucernam; prius declina a malo, et tunc fac bonum; prius quiesce agere perverse, et tunc disce benefacere.
latinJewel.english: First, then, gird your loins, and then light the lamp; first turn away from evil, and then do good; first cease to act perversely, and then learn to do well.
